lessonTitle: Labour and Delivery and Post Partum in Sickle Cell Disease

items.html: Increase awareness of guidance for optimizing birth
Identify and reduce risks in postpartum care

data.html:
Inducing Labour

Based on the American Society of Hematology (ASH) 2020 guidelines, it is recommended that labour be induced between 38 and 40 weeks.

Rationale: to decrease perinatal mortality, particularly for patients with SCD, related to placental abnormalities

items.html: Epidural analgesia is safe and effective for pregnant women with SCD.
Avoid dehydration to reduce risk of sickling crisis
Continuous electronic fetal monitoring due to increased risk of unexplained stillbirth and adverse perinatal outcomes for pregnant women with SCD.

data.html:
Regional anesthesia is preferred for Cesarean section. General anesthesia is associated with higher risks of sickling complications.

Vigilance around pain management is essential in the immediate post partum period. An acute pain crisis after delivery happens in approximately 25% of women. Hydration, oxygenation and early mobilization reduce pain crisis risk.

data.question: Spinal anaesthesia is recommended to reduce post partum VOE.
data.feedback_correct: Regional (spinal) anaesthesia is preferred over general anaesthesia which is associated with higher risks of sickling complications.

items.html: Optimizing intrapartum care is critical to reducing risks postpartum
The postpartum period offers the opportunity to advise about optimization of future pregnancies and contraception.
Camous J, N'da A, Etienne-Julan M, Stéphan F. Anesthetic management of pregnant women with sickle cell disease--effect on postnatal sickling complications. Can J Anaesth. 2008 May;55(5):276-83
Berghella, V. (Ed.). (2017). Maternal-Fetal Evidence Based Guidelines (3rd ed.). CRC Press
